codeReview.summary: The worker audited epub_output.py (1504 lines) against Go counterparts (kfx.go 605 lines, render.go 598 lines, html.go 432 lines, epub/epub.go 789 lines). The commit only modifies a comment in sectionXHTML explaining xmlns:epub design rationale and adds audit findings documentation. No functional code changes were made. The audit correctly identified the xmlns:epub mechanism: Python creates all XHTML with xmlns:epub but lxml's cleanup_namespaces strips it; Go mirrors this by conditionally adding it. 394/394 comparison passes. However, the audit's claim of 'all 58 branches traced' and '✅ Parity' for create_opf is overstated — many Python branches in create_opf have no Go counterpart because Go's contentOPF omits entire metadata categories (subject, rights, fixed_layout, orientation_lock, writing_mode, scrolled_continuous, book_type, region_magnification, virtual_panels, illustrated_layout, html_cover, guided_view_native, aspect ratios, dictionary, magazine x-metadata). These fields also aren't passed from decodedBook to epub.Book. Similarly, consolidate_html's div-collapsing is significantly simplified in Go versus Python. The audit should have flagged these more explicitly rather than marking them as parity. That said, the test books (394/394) confirm these gaps are not triggered by current fixtures, and the worker did note 4 non-blocking issues.

issues.description: The audit claims 'All 58 branches traced' and '✅ Parity' for create_opf → contentOPF, but Go's contentOPF (epub.go:286-480) omits approximately 20+ Python branches: subject (L933-934), rights (L937-938), fixed_layout metadata (L941-951), scrolled_continuous (L953-954), book_type (L956-957), orientation_lock (L959-964), writing_mode (L966-972), region_magnification (L974-975), virtual_panels (L977-981), illustrated_layout (L983-984), html_cover (L986-987), guided_view_native (L989-990), aspect ratios (L992-998), is_dictionary (L1000-1007), is_magazine (L1009-1013), prefix declaration (L1015-1016), EPUB2 mimetype mapping (L1027), cover-image handling (L1033-1034), FXL properties (L1036-1045), spine itemref properties (L1067-1075), linear=no (L1077-1078). The audit should have listed these as non-blocking gaps rather than claiming parity. Fields like OrientationLock, WritingMode, FixedLayout exist in decodedBook but are not passed to epub.Book at kfx.go:235-250.

The consolidate_html div-collapsing audit (L768-815 → cleanupHTMLParts) is incomplete. Python checks parent tag (aside, caption, div, figure, h1-h6, li, p, td, IDX_ENTRY) AND body with child tag validation before collapsing. Go's shouldCollapseNestedDiv (render.go:589-599) only handles the simple case: div with one child div (both no attrs). The Python also handles body-parent divs with block-level-only children. The audit marks this as ✅ Parity* but should explicitly note the div-collapsing scope difference, not just the adjacent same-tag merging.

The xmlns:epub detection via `strings.Contains(section.BodyHTML, 'epub:type="')` is a heuristic that could miss edge cases (e.g., epub:type with single quotes, or namespace-prefixed variants). Python uses lxml's cleanup_namespaces which tracks actual namespace usage. The current approach works for all test books, but is less robust than Python's approach.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The epub.Book struct (epub.go:25-62) has IllustratedLayout, FixedLayout, and PageProgressionDirection fields, but these are never populated when constructing epub.Book from decodedBook (kfx.go:235-250, 278-293, 412-427, 451-466). Additionally, many fields present in Python's EPUB_Output class (subject, rights, orientation_lock, writing_mode, scrolled_continuous, region_magnification, etc.) have no equivalent in epub.Book at all. This means contentOPF cannot emit their metadata even if they were populated. The audit findings document doesn't clearly call out this structural gap.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: kfx.go:235-250 shows epub.Book construction without OrientationLock, WritingMode, FixedLayout fields. epub/epub.go Book struct lacks Subject, Rights, ScrolledContinuous, BookType, OrientationLock, WritingMode, RegionMagnification, etc.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Audit findings documents (.factory/library/audit-*-findings.md) use ✅ Parity markers liberally even when significant branches are unimplemented but simply not triggered by test books. This could mislead future workers into thinking the code has full coverage when it actually has significant gaps for uncommon book types (magazines, dictionaries, fixed layout, illustrated layout, comics). A more accurate convention would be ✅ Parity (current test books only) or ⚠️ Parity (partial).
sharedStateObservations.evidence: audit-epub-output-findings.md marks create_opf as '✅ Parity' with 'All 58 branches traced' despite ~20+ branches having no Go counterpart. consolidate_html div-collapsing marked as '✅ Parity*' despite simplified scope.

summary: The audit correctly identified that the 5 assertions (VAL-EPUB-001 through VAL-EPUB-016) are satisfied for current test books (394/394 match). The xmlns:epub mechanism is well-documented. The commit is documentation-only (comment improvement + findings doc). However, the audit overclaims parity: create_opf has ~20+ branches with no Go counterpart (fixed_layout, orientation_lock, dictionary, magazine metadata, etc.), and consolidate_html's div-collapsing logic is significantly simplified. These gaps don't affect the 6 test books but would impact magazines, dictionaries, fixed-layout books, illustrated books, and comics. The worker noted 4 non-blocking issues but should have flagged the broader OPF metadata gap more explicitly. Since the task was to audit and the test results confirm current books pass, this is a pass with noted accuracy concerns in the audit documentation.
